topic: A person discovers their child has the same teacher they had
raw_output:
[story 1]

The envelope felt heavy in Sarah’s hand, the embossed seal of Northwood Elementary stark against the cream paper. Inside, a letter, formal and impersonal, informed her that her son, Leo, was enrolled in the same third-grade class as her own child, Maya. Sarah reread the names, the dates, the shared classroom number, the sudden, sharp intake of breath catching in her throat. She traced the familiar script of the teacher’s signature, a hand she hadn't seen in nearly a decade. A strange, hollow feeling settled in her chest, like a stone dropped into deep water. She walked to the window, staring out at the busy street, the noise of traffic suddenly grating and irrelevant. How could this be? It felt like a trick of the light, a scenario too improbable to settle into reality.

[story 2]

Mark stood by the kitchen counter, the lukewarm coffee forgotten in his hand. The school newsletter had landed on the table, and his eyes kept snagging on the familiar photo of Mrs. Davison. It was a flicker of recognition, quickly followed by a dizzying sense of dislocation. He leaned against the granite, his shoulders slumping slightly. He picked up the laminated roster, his fingers trembling just enough to make the edges crinkle. Every name seemed to align, every grade level a perfectly mirrored reflection of his own past. A low murmur escaped his lips, a sound that caught in his throat. He shook his head slowly, trying to dislodge the unsettling pattern, but the image of that teacher, standing in that classroom, refused to dissolve.

[story 3]

When the school notification arrived, David felt a sudden, intense pressure behind his eyes. He stared at the printed confirmation, his gaze drifting from the familiar teacher's name to the picture of his own daughter, Lily, smiling brightly in the accompanying photograph. A cold sweat prickled his hairline. He paced the length of his living room three times, his movements jerky and uncoordinated. He stopped abruptly, planting his hands on his thighs, his breathing shallow and rapid. "This isn't right," he managed to whisper, the sound thin and brittle. He looked down at his hands, flexing his fingers as if checking if they were still his own, utterly unable to reconcile the present situation with the deep, unsettling echo of the past.
